frame_analyses.description: Title slide: 'Laparoscopic Roaders Knot' by Prof. Dr. R. K. Mishra, Laparoscopy Hospital, New Delhi, India. This is an educational introduction.
Suture (white silk) being held by two hands outside the trainer. Single strand visible, approximately 90cm length estimated.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Clean suture presentation, proper hand positioning for knot tying demonstration

frame_analyses.description: First knot formation begins. Left hand grasps the suture end, right hand begins wrapping to form first throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Clean, controlled hand positions. Demonstrates proper tension control.

frame_analyses.description: First throw in progress. Loop being formed around the working end of the suture.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Smooth wrapping motion, good control of both strands

frame_analyses.description: First throw nearly complete. Loop formed and positioning for cinching visible.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Proper loop geometry for a single throw

frame_analyses.description: First throw being tightened. Both hands pulling on opposite strands to cinch the knot.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Demonstrates proper tension application without excessive force

frame_analyses.description: First throw appears set. Clean, square knot formation visible at the junction of the two suture ends.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Knot appears secure and properly formed

frame_analyses.description: Second throw being initiated. Left hand repositioned to begin second throw formation.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Smooth transition from first to second throw

frame_analyses.description: Second throw in formation. Loop wrapping around suture strand visible.
Second throw being cinched. Both hands working to tighten the knot.
Second throw secured. Two completed throws now visible at knot site.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Knot stack building properly, no slippage observed

frame_analyses.description: Third throw being initiated. Left hand begins final throw formation.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Proper hand positioning for third throw

frame_analyses.description: Third throw loop formation in progress.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Consistent with prior throws, clean technique

frame_analyses.description: Third throw being cinched. Final tightening of the knot structure.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Appropriate tension applied to secure final throw

frame_analyses.description: All three throws completed and secured. Complete knot stack visible.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Knot appears well-formed, three distinct throws visible, no slippage

frame_analyses.description: Penrose drain with tied suture visible. The foam appendage shows the completed knot at the intended closure site.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Knot positioned correctly on the drain structure

frame_analyses.description: Close-up of the completed knot on the drain. Slit closure appears complete and tension is maintained.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Drain slit closed properly around the knot, no visible gap

knot_assessments.notes: Clean, controlled throw with proper loop formation and cinching. No slippage observed.
Smooth transition from first throw. Second throw equally well-formed with appropriate tension.
Final throw completes knot stack. All three throws maintain integrity without visible slippage.

suture_placement.notes: This is an expert demonstration of knot tying technique on a bench model. Specific target mark placement on a Penrose drain is not shown in the video—the demonstration focuses on the mechanics of forming and securing extracorporeal knots. No suture passages through marked targets are visible.

drain_assessment.notes: Final frames show the tied knot seated on the foam appendage with the slit closed. No visible gap or tissue damage.

confidence_rationale: This is clearly an expert demonstration video by Prof. Dr. R. K. Mishra focused on teaching the mechanics of laparoscopic knot tying. The video demonstrates flawless technique in forming and cinching three extracorporeal single throws. However, the video does not show the complete FLS task4 workflow (suture passage through two marked targets on a Penrose drain inside a trainer box, followed by knot advancement with a knot pusher and suture cutting). Instead, it isolates the knot-tying mechanics on a bench model. The completion time is artificially short because it begins with the suture already outside the trainer. If this were scored as a full task4 performance, the time would represent only the knot-tying component. The knot quality, hand switching, and tension control are all expert-level.
cannot_determine: Actual suture placement deviation from marked targets—no targets are shown or referenced in the demonstration
Whether a knot pusher was used to advance the knots—the video shows hand tying only
Whether suture ends were cut inside the trainer—the demonstration ends after knot completion
task_completion_notes: This video demonstrates the extracorporeal knot-tying component of FLS task4 only. The complete task requires: (1) suture introduction into the trainer, (2) passage through two marked targets, (3) extracorporeal knot formation, (4) knot advancement with knot pusher, and (5) suture cutting inside the trainer. This video shows step 3 only.
technique_summary: Expert demonstration of extracorporeal single-throw knot tying technique. All three throws are formed cleanly with proper loop geometry, controlled tension, and no slippage. Hand switching between throws is smooth and intentional. The knot stack is symmetric and appears mechanically sound. This is representative of proficiency-level performance for the knot-tying component of the task.
strengths: All three throws formed with clean, controlled technique
Proper hand switching between throws (right → left → right)
No visible knot slippage or loosening
Appropriate tension applied to each throw—not excessive, sufficient to secure
Clear demonstration of knot stack geometry

demonstrated_techniques: Loop formation with leading hand wrapping around standing end
Proper hand positioning to maintain control of both suture strands
Hand alternation for sequential throws (leading hand switch)
Appropriate tension control to avoid knot slippage
common_errors_addressed: Video implicitly demonstrates avoidance of loose knot formation through consistent cinching
Video shows proper tension to prevent slippage and knot failure
Clean loop formations avoid the error of twisted or malformed throws
teaching_content.instructional_value: This video serves as an excellent reference for the mechanics of forming three secure extracorporeal single throws. It isolates the knot-tying skill from the full FLS task context, making it useful for trainees focusing on knot technique before attempting the complete task.

video_type_notes: Expert demonstration video by Prof. Dr. R. K. Mishra titled 'Laparoscopic Roaders Knot.' The video is not a full FLS task4 performance evaluation but rather a focused technical demonstration of knot-tying mechanics on a bench model. It is suitable for educational purposes and illustrates proficiency-level technique for the knot component of the task.
